content.text: When a language model predicts the next token, it produces a probability distribution over an entire vocabulary that can contain tens of thousands of candidates. Allowing unconstrained sampling from that full distribution—especially at elevated temperatures—risks selecting low-quality or incoherent tokens. Top-k and top-p (nucleus) sampling are two complementary truncation strategies that restrict which tokens are eligible before drawing the final sample, giving practitioners fine-grained control over the creativity–coherence trade-off.
What it is

Top-k sampling keeps only the k tokens with the highest probability at each generation step, discarding the rest before sampling. If k = 40, the model can only choose among the 40 most likely next tokens no matter how the probabilities are distributed. The value k is a fixed integer, so the candidate pool is always exactly k tokens wide—regardless of whether the distribution is sharply peaked or nearly flat.

Top-p sampling, introduced by Holtzman et al. (2020) under the name "nucleus sampling," takes a different approach: it builds the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability meets or exceeds the threshold p, then samples from that set. When the model is highly confident (one token dominates), the nucleus might contain just a handful of candidates; when the model is uncertain, the nucleus expands to include many tokens. This adaptive behavior is the core advantage of top-p over top-k.

Both parameters operate after the raw logits are converted to probabilities (via softmax) and after any temperature scaling has been applied. They are not mutually exclusive—most inference engines apply top-k first to cap the pool, then apply top-p to further trim that pool, and finally sample from whatever remains.
Why it matters

Without truncation, a high temperature makes the distribution almost uniform, letting the model sample extremely rare tokens—grammatically correct but semantically bizarre words, stray punctuation, or tokens that break structured formats like JSON or XML. Even at moderate temperatures the long tail of the vocabulary introduces noise.

For agentic AI systems this is particularly critical. An agent that generates a tool call must produce valid JSON; a single sampled garbage token can corrupt the entire payload and cause a downstream error. A retrieval-augmented system that formats a query must stay within expected syntax. Nucleus sampling acts as a probability mass filter: it throws away the tokens that the model itself assigns negligible probability to, preventing fringe selections that would not survive stricter decoding.

Top-p also addresses the main weakness of top-k: when the model is very confident (say 99% probability mass on one token), top-k still permits 39 other largely irrelevant candidates, diluting the signal. Top-p would reduce the nucleus to one or two tokens in that scenario, matching the model's own certainty with appropriately constrained sampling.
Benefits
Eliminates garbage tokens
— Both strategies prevent ultra-low-probability tokens from ever being selected, keeping outputs coherent.
Adaptive nucleus width (top-p)
— The candidate pool expands when the model is uncertain and contracts when it is confident, mirroring the model's own calibration.
Predictable diversity (top-k)
— A fixed k gives a hard upper bound on the number of candidates, which is useful when reproducibility and stability matter more than semantic richness.
Composable with temperature
— Temperature shifts the shape of the distribution; top-k and top-p then prune it. The three parameters together give fine-grained control without model retraining.
Low computational overhead
— Both operations are O(V log V) where V is vocabulary size, dominated by the sort step that most inference kernels already perform for greedy decoding.
Trade-offs

Top-k has one fundamental weakness: it ignores the shape of the distribution. A k of 50 includes 50 tokens whether the first token holds 95% of the mass or whether it is spread evenly. In the confident case, k = 50 is far too permissive; in the uncertain case, k = 50 might cut off genuinely reasonable candidates. This makes top-k's behavior inconsistent across different contexts within a single conversation.

Top-p is more adaptive but introduces its own non-linearity: small changes in p near 1.0 can dramatically change how many tokens are eligible when the distribution is nearly flat. Setting p = 0.99 versus p = 1.0 might include or exclude hundreds of tokens depending on the context. Debugging why a particular generation went wrong is harder when the candidate pool size varies at every step.

Neither parameter is a substitute for temperature. Setting top-p = 0.1 with temperature = 1.5 still samples from a chaotic distribution; the nucleus will just be small but wildly re-ranked. The parameters are most useful when calibrated together. Tuning them independently without a systematic evaluation harness often leads to local optima that do not generalize across tasks.
Top-k ignores distribution shape — behaves differently under confident vs. uncertain contexts.
Top-p has non-linear sensitivity near p = 1.0.
API exposure varies — OpenAI exposes top_p but not top_k on hosted models; local stacks expose both.
Interaction effects — tuning temperature, top-k, and top-p simultaneously without ablations is error-prone.
How it works
The generation pipeline

At each decoding step, the model outputs a vector of raw scores—logits—one per vocabulary token. These are unnormalized and can be any real number. Temperature scaling divides every logit by T before the softmax step: lower T sharpens the distribution (the highest-scoring token dominates more), higher T flattens it (scores become more equal).

After softmax, the probabilities are sorted in descending order. Top-k then hard-clips this sorted list to the first k entries, setting all others to zero, followed by re-normalization. Top-p scans the sorted list and includes tokens until their cumulative sum reaches or exceeds p:

attrs.latex: S_p = \min \left\{ S \subseteq V : \sum_{i \in S} p_i \geq p \right\}

content.text: where S is taken to be the top tokens in sorted order. After pruning, the remaining probabilities are re-normalized so they sum to 1, and a single token is drawn via multinomial (categorical) sampling. The drawn token is appended to the context and the process repeats for the next position.
When both top-k and top-p are active

When a stack applies both parameters, top-k runs first: tokens outside the top k are eliminated regardless of p. Then top-p trims what remains. The effective candidate pool is the intersection: the set that is both within the top k and within the probability nucleus. This ordering means top-k acts as a hard cap and top-p acts as a softer adaptive filter within that cap. A common default in llama.cpp is top_k=40, top_p=0.95.
Architecture
The diagram below illustrates the full token sampling pipeline, contrasting how top-k and top-p each filter the probability distribution before the final sample is drawn.

attrs.alt: Token sampling pipeline showing top-k and top-p filtering stages

content.text: When to use it
Recommended scenarios
Creative text generation (stories, marketing copy)
— top_p=0.9 with temperature=0.8 gives diversity while cutting the garbage tail.
Conversational agents
— top_p=0.95, temperature=0.7 is a widely used baseline for natural, varied replies.
Local inference with llama.cpp or Ollama
— Both top_k and top_p are exposed; combine them (top_k=40, top_p=0.9) as a starting baseline.
Diversity sampling in evaluation harnesses
— When generating multiple candidate answers to measure self-consistency, nucleus sampling produces diverse but plausible alternatives.
When NOT to use these filters
Structured outputs (JSON, code, SQL)
— Set temperature=0 (or very low) and let greedy/beam decoding handle token selection. Top-p with even moderate temperature can corrupt format.
Deterministic tool calls in agentic pipelines
— A function name or argument value must be exact; sampling parameters add unnecessary variance.
When the provider does not support the parameter
— OpenAI's GPT-4o does not expose top_k; passing it has no effect (or causes an API error on some endpoints).
How to implement

Implementation involves two layers: calling a provider API with the right parameters, and—for local stacks or custom inference—implementing the sampling logic directly. The latter is useful for understanding and for writing custom inference servers.
Step 1 — Implement nucleus sampling from scratch (educational)

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def sample_token(logits: torch.Tensor, temperature: float = 1.0,
                 top_k: int = 0, top_p: float = 1.0) -> int:
    """
    Draw one token from logits with optional temperature, top-k, and top-p.

    Args:
        logits:      Raw model output, shape (vocab_size,)
        temperature: Scale factor applied before softmax (>0).
        top_k:       If > 0, restrict to top-k tokens. 0 = disabled.
        top_p:       Nucleus threshold (0, 1]. 1.0 = no nucleus filter.

    Returns:
        Integer token index.
    """
    # 1. Apply temperature
    if temperature != 1.0:
        logits = logits / temperature

    # 2. Convert to probabilities
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)

    # 3. Sort descending
    sorted_probs, sorted_indices = torch.sort(probs, descending=True)

    # 4. Top-k: zero out everything beyond rank k
    if top_k > 0:
        sorted_probs[top_k:] = 0.0

    # 5. Top-p: find the nucleus and zero out the tail
    if top_p < 1.0:
        cumulative = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=0)
        # Keep tokens up to and including the one that crosses p
        # (shift cumsum by 1 so the crossing token is included)
        remove = (cumulative - sorted_probs) >= top_p
        sorted_probs[remove] = 0.0

    # 6. Re-normalize
    if sorted_probs.sum() == 0:
        # Fallback: pick the top token
        return sorted_indices[0].item()
    sorted_probs = sorted_probs / sorted_probs.sum()

    # 7. Sample
    sampled_rank = torch.multinomial(sorted_probs, num_samples=1).item()
    return sorted_indices[sampled_rank].item()


# --- Demo ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    torch.manual_seed(42)
    vocab_size = 100
    # Simulate a logit vector with one dominant token
    logits = torch.randn(vocab_size) * 2
    logits[7] += 5  # token 7 is clearly favored

    for _ in range(5):
        tok = sample_token(logits, temperature=0.8, top_k=40, top_p=0.9)
        print(f"sampled token: {tok}")
Step 2 — Call a hosted API with top_p

import os
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a creative writing assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Write the opening sentence of a science fiction story."},
    ],
    temperature=0.85,
    top_p=0.92,       # nucleus: allow ~92% of the probability mass
    max_tokens=80,
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)

Note: OpenAI recommends altering either temperature or top_p but not both in the same request. In practice many teams use top_p=1.0 (disabled) and vary temperature, or fix temperature=1.0 and vary top_p; mixing both makes ablations harder.
Examples
Example 1 — Minimal nucleus sampling in NumPy (no ML framework)

import numpy as np

def nucleus_sample(probs: np.ndarray, p: float = 0.9, rng=None) -> int:
    """
    Pure-NumPy nucleus (top-p) sampler.

    probs: 1-D array of probabilities summing to 1.
    p:     Nucleus threshold.
    Returns the sampled token index.
    """
    if rng is None:
        rng = np.random.default_rng()

    # Sort descending
    order = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]
    sorted_p = probs[order]

    # Find nucleus
    cumsum = np.cumsum(sorted_p)
    # Include the first token that pushes cumsum >= p
    cut = np.searchsorted(cumsum, p, side="left") + 1
    nucleus_indices = order[:cut]
    nucleus_probs = sorted_p[:cut]
    nucleus_probs = nucleus_probs / nucleus_probs.sum()  # re-normalize

    chosen = rng.choice(nucleus_indices, p=nucleus_probs)
    return int(chosen)


# Quick test with a toy vocabulary of 8 tokens
if __name__ == "__main__":
    rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
    probs = np.array([0.40, 0.25, 0.15, 0.08, 0.05, 0.04, 0.02, 0.01])
    counts = {i: 0 for i in range(len(probs))}
    for _ in range(10_000):
        counts[nucleus_sample(probs, p=0.85, rng=rng)] += 1

    print("Token : count (top-p=0.85, 10k samples)")
    for tok, cnt in counts.items():
        bar = "#" * (cnt // 100)
        print(f"  {tok}: {cnt:5d}  {bar}")
    # Tokens 0-2 (cumsum=0.80) plus token 3 (cumsum=0.88 >= 0.85) form the nucleus
    # Tokens 4-7 should have 0 counts
Example 2 — Local server via llama.cpp REST API with top-k and top-p

import requests
import json

LLAMA_URL = "http://localhost:8080/completion"

def generate(prompt: str, top_k: int = 40, top_p: float = 0.95,
             temperature: float = 0.7, max_tokens: int = 200) -> str:
    payload = {
        "prompt": prompt,
        "n_predict": max_tokens,
        "temperature": temperature,
        "top_k": top_k,
        "top_p": top_p,
        "stop": ["\n\n"],  # Stop at blank line
    }
    r = requests.post(LLAMA_URL, json=payload, timeout=60)
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()["content"]


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Creative mode: wider nucleus, moderate temperature
    creative = generate(
        "List three unusual applications of quantum computing:",
        top_k=50, top_p=0.95, temperature=0.8,
    )
    print("=== Creative (top_k=50, top_p=0.95, T=0.8) ===")
    print(creative)

    # Focused mode: tight nucleus, low temperature
    focused = generate(
        "List three unusual applications of quantum computing:",
        top_k=10, top_p=0.6, temperature=0.3,
    )
    print("\n=== Focused (top_k=10, top_p=0.6, T=0.3) ===")
    print(focused)
Real-world example

A retrieval-augmented customer support agent processes thousands of queries per day. The pipeline retrieves relevant knowledge-base snippets, injects them into a prompt, and generates a reply. The team found that with temperature=0.9 and top_p=1.0 (no nucleus filter), around 2% of responses contained garbled sentences where the model sampled improbable tokens mid-reply—particularly after rare proper nouns. Setting top_p=0.92 with the same temperature reduced the garbled-response rate to under 0.1% in a 7-day A/B test across 50,000 requests, with no measurable impact on human satisfaction scores.
The team's configuration for their production vLLM deployment:

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

llm = LLM(model="mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2")

# Production settings for customer support responses
sampling_params = SamplingParams(
    temperature=0.9,
    top_p=0.92,
    top_k=50,          # Hard cap: never more than 50 candidates
    max_tokens=512,
    stop=["<|im_end|>", "\n\n---"],
)

prompts = [
    "[INST] Our product stopped syncing after the last update. [/INST]",
]

outputs = llm.generate(prompts, sampling_params)
for output in outputs:
    print(output.outputs[0].text)

The combination of top_k=50 and top_p=0.92 ensures the nucleus never exceeds 50 tokens and further trims it to the 92% probability mass within those 50—an intersection that stabilized output quality without reducing perceived creativity.
Common mistakes
Tuning temperature and top_p simultaneously without ablations

Changing temperature from 0.7 to 0.9 while simultaneously lowering top_p from 0.95 to 0.8 makes it impossible to attribute any quality change to either parameter. Always hold one constant while varying the other.
Applying nucleus sampling to structured outputs

# WRONG: nucleus sampling on JSON generation
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role":"user","content":"Return {name: string, age: number} for Alice, 30"}],
    temperature=0.8,
    top_p=0.9,   # Invites random tokens inside the JSON value
)

# RIGHT: temperature=0 for deterministic structured output
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role":"user","content":"Return {name: string, age: number} for Alice, 30"}],
    temperature=0,
    top_p=1.0,   # Disabled; greedy decoding at temp=0
)
Assuming top_k is available on all APIs

OpenAI's Chat Completions and Responses APIs do not expose top_k. Passing it as an extra keyword argument either raises a validation error or is silently ignored, depending on the SDK version. Check provider documentation before including top_k in production code.
Setting top_p very low with high temperature

Top-p = 0.3 with temperature = 1.5 means: first widen the distribution with high temperature, then restrict to only 30% of the mass. The result is that the 30% mass window contains far fewer tokens than it would at low temperature—effectively forcing the model into a very small set of candidates drawn from a chaotic ranking. This is almost never the intended behavior.
Best practices
Choose one primary lever
— Either tune temperature with top_p=1.0, or fix temperature=1.0 and tune top_p. Mixing both makes evaluation harder.
Start with provider defaults
— For OpenAI models, temperature=0.7, top_p=1.0 is a safe starting point for conversational tasks.
Use temperature=0 for structured outputs
— JSON, SQL, and tool call arguments benefit from greedy decoding; sampling adds no value and introduces risk.
Record effective settings per model
— Llama-3 8B, Mistral 7B, and GPT-4o can require different calibration for the same task. Keep a configuration registry.
A/B test on real traffic
— Human preference studies often show surprisingly small differences between top_p=0.9 and top_p=0.95 at the same temperature; measure before optimizing.
Combine top_k + top_p for local models
— A good baseline for llama.cpp or Ollama: top_k=40, top_p=0.9, temperature=0.7. Adjust from there.
Log the sampling parameters with every generation
— When debugging an output that went wrong, you need to know the exact combination of temperature, top_p, and top_k that produced it.
Performance & scalability

The computational cost of top-k and top-p filtering is negligible relative to the forward pass of a transformer. The dominant cost is sorting the logit vector: O(V log V) where V is vocabulary size (typically 32k–128k tokens). Modern inference kernels implement top-k with a partial sort (heap select) that runs in O(V + k log k), reducing overhead further.

At batch inference scale—serving hundreds of requests per second on a GPU cluster—the sampling step accounts for under 1% of total latency. vLLM and TGI (Text Generation Inference) batch the sampling computation across the entire request batch, making it effectively free at scale.

One scaling consideration unique to top-p: the nucleus size varies per position and per sequence in the batch, which can complicate batched sampling implementations. Some kernels pad the nucleus to a fixed size per batch to enable vectorized operations, trading a small amount of correctness for throughput. This is invisible to API users but matters when implementing custom inference servers.

For speculative decoding—a technique where a small draft model proposes tokens that a large model verifies—the sampling parameters of the draft model must match those of the target model to avoid acceptance rate degradation. Mismatched top-p values between draft and target are a common source of poor speculative decoding performance.
Tooling & ecosystem
Hosted APIs
OpenAI (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini)
— Exposes top_p (0–1). Does not expose top_k. Parameter name: top_p.
Anthropic (Claude 3.x)
— Exposes top_p and top_k. Parameter names: top_p, top_k.
Google (Gemini)
— Exposes topP and topK. Note the camelCase naming in the Gemini API.
Mistral AI
— Exposes top_p. Does not expose top_k on the hosted API.
Local inference stacks

# llama.cpp REST server
curl http://localhost:8080/completion \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "prompt": "The capital of France is",
    "temperature": 0.7,
    "top_k": 40,
    "top_p": 0.9,
    "n_predict": 50
  }'

# Ollama Python library
# pip install ollama
python3 - <<'EOF'
import ollama
resp = ollama.generate(
    model="llama3",
    prompt="The capital of France is",
    options={"temperature": 0.7, "top_k": 40, "top_p": 0.9},
)
print(resp["response"])
EOF
HuggingFace Transformers

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

model_id = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16)

inputs = tokenizer("The quick brown fox", return_tensors="pt")

outputs = model.generate(
    **inputs,
    max_new_tokens=50,
    do_sample=True,       # Must be True for top-p / top-k
    temperature=0.8,
    top_k=40,
    top_p=0.9,
)
print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

Security & reliability
Prompt injection and adversarial inputs

Top-p and top-k do not provide defenses against prompt injection. An adversarial user can craft inputs that steer the model toward harmful tokens even if those tokens lie in a narrow nucleus. Sampling parameters shape the distribution of plausible completions; they do not filter for content policy compliance. Output filtering, constitutional AI techniques, or a separate classifier layer are needed for content safety.
Reproducibility and audit trails

Even with a fixed seed, reproducing a stochastic output requires logging the exact temperature, top_p, top_k, and seed values that were active at inference time. Systems that adjust these parameters dynamically (e.g., based on task type or user tier) must include the effective values in their audit log alongside each generation. Without this, debugging production failures becomes extremely difficult.
Failure modes at boundary values
top_p = 0 or top_k = 1
— Collapses to greedy decoding. Repetition loops become more likely for models without repetition penalties.
top_p = 1 + top_k = vocab_size
— No truncation; equivalent to pure temperature sampling. Suitable only at very low temperatures.
Malformed logits (NaN or Inf)
— If the softmax input contains NaN, the entire probability vector can become NaN, and nucleus sampling will either panic or sample uniformly. Always validate logits in custom inference code.
Key takeaways
Top-k restricts sampling to the k most probable tokens (fixed pool); top-p restricts to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability meets threshold p (adaptive pool).
Nucleus (top-p) adapts to the model's confidence: a tight pool when one token dominates, a wider pool when the model is uncertain—this is its core advantage over the fixed-width top-k.
Both filters operate after temperature scaling and before the final sample draw; when both are set, top-k runs first as a hard cap and top-p further trims the resulting pool.
For structured outputs (JSON, SQL, tool calls) use temperature=0 and disable both filters; sampling parameters add variance with no benefit when the correct token must be exact.
API support differs by provider: OpenAI exposes top_p only; Anthropic and Google expose both top_k and top_p; local stacks (llama.cpp, vLLM, Ollama) expose both.
Tune one parameter at a time against a fixed evaluation set; changing temperature and top_p simultaneously makes it impossible to attribute quality changes to either knob.
Sampling overhead is negligible at inference scale—under 1% of total latency even in high-throughput serving—so there is no performance cost to using these filters in production.
